# PEO vs EOR: Which Is Better for Startups and First Hires? — HQ Simple

## Summary
Definitive 2026 comparison of PEO vs EOR for early-stage companies, covering the structural co-employment distinction, cost comparison, and concrete decision criteria. Particularly useful because it quantifies the entity-setup cost advantage of EOR (faster to first payday: 5 days vs 30-90 days) and surfaces the mid-year exit problem with PEOs (resets tax wage bases and produces two W-2s).

## Key quotations / statistics
- "PEO (Professional Employer Organization): Creates co-employment; your company remains the legal W-2 employer with your FEIN on tax filings. Requires you to already have a US legal entity in each state where you hire."
- "EOR (Employer of Record): The provider is the legal W-2 employer using their own FEIN; you direct day-to-day work. No existing US entity required."
- "EOR is typically faster and cheaper for first hires" (5 days to first payday for EOR vs 30-90 days for PEO setup)
- "PEO monthly fees average $116/month ($1,395/year), while US EOR list prices cluster around $499-$699 monthly"
- "EOR exit is lower cost/effort; PEO mid-year exit resets tax wage bases and produces two W-2s"
- "The IRS has a 3-year statute of limitations for assessing additional tax and penalties, extending to 6 years for substantial omissions" [misclassification risk]
- Choose EOR if: making first hire, hiring across multiple states, hiring internationally, testing a role with uncertain longevity, need to start in days
- Choose PEO if: stable US team, established state payroll registrations, workers' comp and sufficient headcount for pooled benefits, willing to wait 30-90 days for setup

## Annotations for stinger-forge
- The decision criteria list (EOR vs PEO) maps directly to the classification matrix in `guides/02-classification-matrix.md` — use verbatim as the EOR vs PEO fork in the decision tree
- The PEO mid-year exit problem (two W-2s, reset tax wage bases) is a key risk to surface in `guides/07-migration-playbook.md` under "timing a PEO exit"
- The 5-day-to-first-payday EOR stat vs 30-90 days PEO setup is a compelling argument for EOR for first hires — use in `guides/02-classification-matrix.md` EOR recommendation rationale
- Note that the $116/month PEO average is the platform fee only — benefits, workers' comp, and other co-employment pass-throughs add substantially to total cost; this needs a footnote in any cost comparison
- The IRS 3-year / 6-year statute of limitations is essential for the misclassification risk section in `guides/06-compliance-hotspots.md`
